input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin system is a serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes from MABI Robotic AG, featuring a drive train comprising motors, gear transmissions, and cycloidal gears, controlled by a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller. This system operates in high-precision industrial manufacturing environments where accurate motion control and real-time monitoring are critical, making it suitable for digital twinning to enhance operational efficiency and predictive maintenance.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time visualization via dashboard interfaces, continuous multi-physics-aware identification of joint friction for predictive maintenance, and motion accuracy optimization through closed-loop control. These services target manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel, enabling data-driven decision-making, failure prediction, and performance enhancement of the physical robot system.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation utilizes Asset Administration Shells (AAS) for standardized asset modeling, SysML for model-based systems engineering across requirements, functional, and logical architecture layers, MathWorks Simulink for multi-physics simulation, MQTT protocol for real-time sensor data streaming, and RFLP methodology for hierarchical system decomposition. Additionally, OOSEM and SPES frameworks are employed for structured functional and logical architecture development.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system employs a UML class diagram-based Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) with components including Asset, Model, Processing, and DataTrace. Key associations are Asset has DataTrace, Processing uses Model, and DataTrace originates from Processing. The concrete realization in Section IV specifies these elements and their relationships for a specific use case, ensuring traceability of data traces back to their generating processing components and models.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Data exchange occurs via MQTT protocol for sensor data streaming, integration with Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl controller as an external system, and implementation of Asset Administration Shells (AAS) to ensure interoperability with enterprise systems.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) employs SysML for system structure and behavior modeling across abstraction levels (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, product/technical layer). Architecture Description Languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, and SysML IBDs describe components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral aspects are modeled via MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, while analytical models utilize MathWorks Simulink, simulation tools, and general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java). Data sources include contextual data traces from physical systems, aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor, with a gear transmission followed by a cycloidal gear. The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl CNC controller for precise actuation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure includes two position encoders per drive (motor encoder mounted directly on the motor, secondary encoder behind the gearbox), with all sensor time series streamed via MQTT protocol for real-time data acquisition.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Time series sensor data is transmitted from physical to virtual components via MQTT protocol in a one-way communication flow, consistent with the digital shadow's passive nature where no data is pushed back to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The communication protocol is MQTT; however, details on network topology, bandwidth, latency requirements, reliability and redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support capabilities include deriving friction parameters to enhance robot control accuracy through analytics processing components and user interaction via dashboard visualizations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to industry standards including SysML for model-based systems engineering, AADL for architecture description, ACME for component-based modeling, and MontiArc for architectural descriptions. These standards facilitate interoperability among heterogeneous stakeholders and tools, as referenced in the document's discussion on integrating diverse perspectives [31].
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The documents reference a physical safety cell as a safety measure within the automation pyramid framework, but do not elaborate on cybersecurity protocols, access controls, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, risk assessment approaches, or safety-critical system considerations.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the specific robot model, its components, and controller. It captures the hardware details found in Section III, including the MABI MAX 100, motor, gears, and Siemens controller. However, the description of the operational purpose slightly extrapolates by mentioning 'predictive maintenance' and 'operational efficiency', which are not explicitly stated as the primary goals in the text (which focuses on 'motion accuracy' and 'machining').

The extraction correctly mentions visualization via dashboard and friction identification, which are supported by the text. However, it makes several unsupported claims regarding target users ('manufacturing engineers and maintenance personnel') and specific service capabilities like 'failure prediction' and 'data-driven decision-making', which are not found in the source documents.

The extraction is highly detailed and strongly grounded in the text. It correctly lists the tools (Simulink, MQTT), frameworks (AAS, SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES), and their specific roles as described in the Background and Implementation sections.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

nents. Additional Metadata supports further data processing, particularly in digital twin scenarios. Different types of
models are used depending on the goal: engineering models
describe the system’s structure, properties, or behaviors, while
simulation and data calculation models help compute higherlevel abstractions. A Processing component performs these
computational steps: it consumes (uses) input data traces and
produces output data, which then forms part of the shadow.
The processing logic is encoded in either data calculation or
simulation models, supporting fine-grained traceability [16].
By following the originates from association, one can
trace a data trace back to its generating processing component,
the consumed input traces, and the models involved. If a data
trace is computed using other traces, this traceability remains
intact in a transitive manner.

This DS structure is described as a reference model on

purpose: A digital shadow designer has the flexibility to
concretize the Digital Shadow Reference Model and customize
the DS model to suit their specific use case, as we do in this
paper. _Reference models_ describe concepts of a domain and
their relationships, and they have “ _a conformance relation that_
_precisely identifies the set of conforming models within the_
_same language” [41]_ . In a concretization development step,
one can come up with such a _conforming_ model (a _concrete_
_realization_ or _concretization_ ) for a reference model. Such a
concrete realization _incarnates_ the concepts from the reference model using concrete elements and relations. For Class
Diagrams (CDs), we specify classes and their associations,
conforming to the reference CD, either by choosing similar
names or giving a concrete _conformance relation_, e.g., in the
form of a mapping of incarnations to reference concepts.


_C. How to create Digital Shadows_


To define digital shadows requires collecting all relevant
information for their constituents [38], e.g., which concrete
data traces are needed from which part of a cyber-physical
system (CPS). Within [15], we have sketched a method on how
to create DSs, further refined it with use cases from injection
molding in [42], and integrated it in a method to define digital
twins [43].
There are two ways of designing digital shadows:. The first
option is to come up from the data which is accessible from
the system. The second option is purpose-driven, where we
start from the particular reason and requirements we aim to
create the DS for. The main steps are:

While the abstract mentions 'real-time' and the introduction mentions 'live measurements', these are general descriptors lacking the specific quantitative time scales or synchronization protocols requested.

The description 'Not in Document' is correct. The document focuses on creating a digital shadow for a specific single asset ('the first axis of the MABI robot'). It does not discuss multiple instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between different twins.

The description accurately details the Digital Shadow Reference Model and its concrete realization. It correctly lists the UML-based components (Asset, Model, Processing, DataTrace) and their relationships as defined in the reference model (Fig. 1) and instantiated in Section IV. The text confirms these associations: 'By following the originates from association, one can trace a data trace back to its generating processing component' and 'Processing component... consumes (uses) input data traces and produces output data'.

The description correctly identifies the integration protocols and external systems. The text states data is 'streamed via MQTT' (Section IV.A), the robot is 'controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl' (Section III), and the methodology involves 'implementing Asset Administration Shells (AAS)' (Abstract). These points align perfectly with the extracted characteristic.
